pets.name: Saw
pets.special_needs: Needs more oxygen than other fishes..
pets.characteristics: High-oxygen fish typically include active species such as goldfish, plecos, or fast-swimming cyprinids that require robust aeration and water circulation. Care involves maintaining dissolved oxygen levels through air stones, powerheads, or surface agitation; regular water changes (25–50% weekly) to prevent oxygen depletion; cooler water temperatures when possible, as cold water holds more oxygen; and avoiding overcrowding. Monitor for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement—signs of hypoxia. Diet should be species-appropriate (flakes, pellets, or vegetables depending on type) offered once or twice daily in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Tank size should accommodate activity level and bioload; enrichment includes plants, hiding spots, and open swimming space. Handling is generally not recommended; observe fish behavior and water parameters (pH, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate) regularly.
pets.species_profile_summary: Lifespan varies widely by specific species (3–10 years typical for common high-oxygen fish in captive care); success depends on consistent aeration, water quality, and appropriate tank size.

tasks.name: Feed Saw once daily
tasks.notes: Offer species-appropriate food (flakes, pellets, or vegetables) in amounts consumed within 2–3 minutes. Remove uneaten food to maintain water quality.
tasks.rationale: Consistent feeding supports senior fish health and prevents water quality degradation from excess food.

tasks.name: Monitor Saw's behavior and breathing
tasks.notes: Observe for gasping at the surface, lethargy, or rapid gill movement—signs of low oxygen. Note energy level and appetite. Keep a brief log.
tasks.rationale: Saw requires more oxygen than typical fish. Daily observation creates a record of appetite and energy, essential for detecting hypoxia or decline early.

tasks.name: Check aeration and water circulation
tasks.notes: Verify air stone, powerhead, or surface agitation is functioning. Ensure dissolved oxygen levels are adequate for Saw's high-oxygen needs.
tasks.rationale: High-oxygen fish depend on robust aeration. Daily checks prevent equipment failure that could cause hypoxia.

tasks.name: Perform 25–50% water change
tasks.notes: Change 25–50% of tank water weekly to prevent oxygen depletion and maintain water parameters (pH, ammonia, nitrite, nitrate).
tasks.rationale: Regular water changes are critical for high-oxygen fish to sustain dissolved oxygen and remove waste that depletes oxygen.

tasks.name: Test water parameters
tasks.notes: Check pH, ammonia, nitrite, and nitrate levels weekly. Record results to track trends.
tasks.rationale: Monitoring water parameters helps detect conditions that reduce oxygen availability and supports early intervention.

tasks.name: Verify tank temperature and oxygen capacity
tasks.notes: Keep water as cool as species tolerates; cooler water holds more dissolved oxygen. Check thermometer weekly.
tasks.rationale: Temperature management is a key strategy for high-oxygen fish. Cooler water naturally supports higher dissolved oxygen levels.

tasks.name: Inspect tank for overcrowding and bioload
tasks.notes: Ensure tank size accommodates Saw's activity level and bioload. Avoid overcrowding, which depletes oxygen. Verify hiding spots and open swimming space are available.
tasks.rationale: Tank size and stocking density directly affect oxygen availability. Monthly review ensures the environment remains suitable for a high-oxygen fish.

tasks.name: Review Saw's health log and report major changes
tasks.notes: Review observations from the past month. If Saw shows sudden decline, refusal of multiple meals, or persistent gasping, contact a veterinarian.
tasks.rationale: Senior fish benefit from consistency and early detection of problems. A monthly review supports proactive care and safe escalation to veterinary guidance when needed.
